content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


September 2, 2003


James Benjamin, Chairman
Half Moon Bay Planning Commission
400 Pilarcitos Avenue
Half Moon Bay, CA 94019-1475

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-03-122

Dear Mr. Benjamin:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Half Moon Bay Planning Commission regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because you cannot provide us with the details of particular decisions actually before the commission, we provide you with informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1.      Does Commissioner McCarthy have a potential conflict of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on vertical trail easements and coastal access pathways within 500 feet of his residential real property?

2.      Do Commissioners Benjamin, McCarthy, Kellenberger, Poncini, Ream and Heinz have potential conflicts of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on environmentally sensitive habitat areas within 500 feet of real property owned by each commissioner?

3.      Does Commissioner Benjamin have a potential conflict of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on unimplemented residential street plan lines within 500 feet of his residential real property?


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (§ 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-03-122
Page No. 2

4.        Does Commissioner Benjamin have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on dam inundation zones within 500
feet of his residential real property?

5.        Does Commissioner McCarthy have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on the unused railroad right-of-way
passing through Half Moon Bay within 500 feet of his residential real property?

6.        Do Commissioners Poncini and Ream have a potential conflict of interest
regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on land use in a tsunami
zone within which they own residential real properties?

7.        Does Commissioner Poncini have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on development and land use within the
residential zoning district and LCP land use planning designations?

8.        Does Commissioner Heinz have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on development and land use within the
agricultural zoning district and LCP land use planning designations?

9.        If one or more of the planning commissioners does have a potential conflict of
interest in the discussions and decisions described above, how should the planning
commission structure its planning process to maximize participation of the planning
commissioners while still adequately addressing conflict concerns?

CONCLUSIONS

Questions 1-8

        We presume that that the planning commissioners whose real property interests
are directly involved in the decisions you describe will have a conflict of interest in those
decisions, unless it can be shown that the decisions will have no financial effect on the
property interest, or that an exception to the conflict of interest rules would apply. From
the facts as presently known, it appears that the officials whose property interests would
be directly involved in these decisions would be prohibited from participating in them.
Therefore, at a minimum, they have "potential" conflicts of interest in these decisions.

Question 9

        We cannot advise you on the optimal structure for the city's planning process,
which requires an understanding of goals and circumstances that can only be supplied by
city officials. To the extent that the legal requirements of the Act may be factors in the
design of your process, the analysis of the first 8 questions may be of some assistance.

File No. 1-03-122
Page No. 3

FACTS

Half Moon Bay is a general law city located along the coast of San Mateo County
and situated entirely within the coastal zone. The city is 6.49 square miles in size and has
a population of 11,842. The city is governed by a city council, which appoints a seven-
member planning commission.

The city is currently preparing to conduct a comprehensive review and update of
its general plan. As part of this update, the city will also be updating its local coastal
program ("LCP"), which consists of the land use element and the city's general plan,
along with implementing zoning ordinances. The land use element presently includes
sections with findings and policies pertaining to coastal access and recreation, environ-
mentally sensitive habitat areas, hazards, housing, archeological and paleontological
resources, visual resources, agriculture, development, specific plans, and public works, as
well as supporting maps and definitions. Other general plan elements include circulation,
parks, safety, and noise. The revised general plan and local coastal program ("GP/LCP")
may change this structure by merging some elements and creating new ones, but specific
organizational changes have not yet been determined.

As part of its duties, the planning commission is responsible for developing and
considering updated policies, maps and ordinances to be included in the revised GP/LCP,
for conducting public hearings on their appropriateness, and recommending policies,
maps and proposed ordinances to the city council. The city council makes the final
determinations on revisions of the GP/LCP, although the council will give the planning
commission's recommendations a great deal of weight.

Due to Half Moon Bay's small size and peculiar geography, it is possible that
GP/LCP revisions will substantially impact property interests within the city. Recent
public dialogue on congestion, growth, geologic hazards, view resources, environ-
mentally sensitive habitat areas, public infrastructure and many other topics suggest that
policies, maps, tables, ordinances, definitions and the tying text may undergo substantial
revision.

The Commissioners' Potentially Affected Economic Interests:

Commissioner Benjamin: Owns residential property located within 500 feet of an
environmentally sensitive habitat area, unimplemented residential street plan lines, and
a dam inundation zone.

Commissioner McCarthy: Owns residential property located within 500 feet of a railroad
right-of-way, a proposed coastal access pathway, and an environmentally sensitive
habitat area.

Commissioner Kellenberger: Owns residential property located within 500 feet of an
environmentally sensitive habitat area.

File No. 1-03-122
Page No. 4

Commissioner Ream: Owns residential property situated within the boundaries of a
"wave 8" or tsunami zone, which is also located within 500 feet of an environmentally
sensitive habitat area.

Commissioner Poncini: Owns residential property located within the boundaries of a
"wave 8" or tsunami zone, which is also within 500 feet of an environmentally sensitive
habitat area. Commissioner Poncini also owns a residentially zoned vacant parcel
adjacent to the previously mentioned residential property.

Commissioner Heinz: Owns real property zoned and designated in the Half Moon Bay
LCP for agricultural use. This property is located within 500 feet of an environmentally
sensitive habitat area.

Commissioner Falcone: There is no indication that Commissioner Falcone has any
economic interests potentially affected by the GP/LCP update.

ANALYSIS

Questions 1 through 8:

The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a "financial interest." (Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official
has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on that
public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of
the official's economic interests. To determine whether a public official has such a
"conflict of interest" in a governmental decision, the FPPC has developed a standard,
eight part analysis outlined at subdivisions 1 through 8 of regulation 18700(b). We apply
that analysis to your first eight questions.

Step One: Are the persons in question "public officials"?

As members of the city's planning commission, the commissioners are public
officials within the meaning of the Act, subject as such to its conflict of interest rules.
(Section 82048; regulation 18701(a).)

Step Two: Will these officials be making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only where a public official
"make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his [or her] official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has reason to
know he [or she] has a financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation 18700(b)(2).) The

File No. I-03-122
Page No. 5

Commission has adopted a series of regulations which define "making," "participating in
making," and "influencing" a governmental decision, which provide as follows:

• A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his
or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.)

• A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening
review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.)

• A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)³

If a public official does not make, participate in making, or use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision, there can be no conflict of interest as
defined under the Act. Your first eight questions all refer to "decisions," and we presume
from this that the planning commissioners will at some point be faced with governmental
decisions relative to each of the topics you describe. Conflicts of interest are therefore
possible, and we may proceed with our analysis. ⁴ As to the "discussions" you expect to
take place as the planning process gets under way, participation in these discussions may
or may not amount to participation in governmental decisions. But for present purposes
it is enough to know that, at some point in the process, the planning commissioners will
confront governmental decisions on the stated topics.

Step Three: What are the possible sources of a conflict of interest for each official?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

³ Regulation 18702.4 lists a series of exceptions to the rules stated in the preceding three
regulations, which apply only in certain limited circumstances.
⁴ If an official has a conflict of interest in a particular decision, regulation 18702.5 explains that
the official is required to leave the room during discussion and voting on the matter in which the official
has a conflict, unless the official wishes to be heard as a member of the general public, as provided in
subdivision (d)(3) of this regulation.

File No. 1-03-122
Page No. 6

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87103(e); regulation 18703.4);

• A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family – this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).

Your account of the facts includes information on various interests in real
property owned by seven of the planning commissioners. For purposes of this letter, we
assume that each of these commissioners has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 in
this real property. You do not suggest that the decisions in question would have a
personal financial effect separate and apart from any effect on the stated real property
interests. Accordingly, "personal financial effects" will not be treated separately from
financial effects on the commissioners' real property interests. (Regulation 18705.5(a).)
Our analysis will be limited, then, to the potential effects of decisionmaking on these real
property interests. 5

Step Four: Are these economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?

Real property is directly involved in a governmental decision if that real property
is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the property
which is the subject of the decision. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).) Your account of the
facts indicates that your own property is located within 500 feet of an environmentally
sensitive habitat area, unimplemented residential street plan lines, and a dam inundation
zone. Accordingly, your real property would be directly involved in governmental
decisions whose subjects include the areas you described.

Similarly, Commissioner McCarthy's property would be directly involved in
decisions on the described railroad right-of-way, proposed coastal access pathway, and
environmentally sensitive habitat area. Commissioner Kellenberger's property would be
directly involved in decisions on the environmentally sensitive habitat area to which you
refer. The property of Commissioners Ream and Poncini would be directly involved in

5 You have not reported any real property interest owned by Planning Commissioner Falcone. But
since Commissioner Falcone does have an economic interest in his personal finances, it will be necessary to
keep in mind any personal financial effects that a decision may have on Commissioner Falcone. You have
not provided us any basis for the analysis of such effects on Commissioner Falcone, so we do not consider
them further in this letter.

File No. I-03-122
Page No. 7

decisions on the tsunami zones and environmentally sensitive habitat areas within 500
feet of their property. Finally, the property of Commissioner Heinz would be directly
involved in decisions on the environmentally sensitive habitat area you described as
situated within 500 feet.

Real property not directly involved in a governmental decision is treated as
indirectly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18704.2(c)(2).) As we understand your
request, you do not seek assistance on decisions in which the commissioners' property is
indirectly involved. We limit our analysis, therefore, to the decisions you describe in
questions 1 through 8. In each of these the real property interest of one commissioner
(at least) is directly involved.

Steps Five and Six: What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon these officials'
economic interests will meet this materiality standard?

Materiality

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) When real property is directly involved in a governmental
decision, there is a presumption that any financial effect of the decision upon the real
property is material. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) and (2).) This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have any
financial effect at all on the real property. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).)

The evidence on which a public official relies to rebut the presumption must be
objectively defensible, that is, based on a full and accurate assessment of reasonable and
objective underlying facts. (Kuhn Advice Letter, No. A-02-192; O'Harra Advice Letter,
No. A-00-174.) You have not furnished us with any evidence tending to rebut a presumption of materiality attaching to the properties found to be directly involved in the
decisions you mention. It may not be possible to provide such evidence at this early
stage; for the present, we must conclude that any financial effects on these properties,
attributable to the decisions you describe, would be material.

Foreseeability

An effect upon an economic interest is considered reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) We have already concluded
(given our present state of knowledge) that any financial effect of the decisions discussed
above, as to property directly involved in the decision, would be material. The question
now is, simply, whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions you have described
will have any financial effect on the directly involved real property. We cannot answer

File No. 1-03-122
Page No. 8

that question because it necessarily depends on facts specific to each decision and to each
parcel of real property, which are not available to us at this time.

Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions.

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in that decision if the circumstances are such that the "public generally"
exception may be invoked. This exception applies when the financial effect of a decision
upon a public official's economic interests is not distinguishable from the effect of the
decision on a significant segment of the public generally. (Section 87103; regulation
18707(a).) We do not have information establishing that a "significant segment" of the
relevant population will be affected by the decision in "substantially the same manner"
as the official is affected, in any of the decisions you describe, and cannot proceed further
in evaluating the possible impact of this exception.

Finally, an official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in that decision if the "legally required participation" exception is applicable.
This is an exception that typically applies when an agency is unable to assemble a
quorum of its members without the participation of an official who has a conflict of
interest. You have not provided any facts suggesting that this exception would apply to
any of the decisions you have described.

In summary, each of the decisions you describe appears to directly involve the
economic interests of at least one planning commissioner, who would have a conflict of
interest in the decision. If it is foreseeable that the decision would have any financial
effect on the directly involved economic interest. At this early stage of the process, we
cannot say more than that.

Question 9

Your final question asks how the planning commission should structure its
planning process to maximize participation of the planning commissioners while
avoiding conflicts of interest. This question presupposes that some commissioners will
have conflicts of interest in some decisions. As suggested by the foregoing analysis, that
is a reasonable supposition. It may be too early to state with certainty that a given
commissioner will have a conflict of interest in a given decision, because the process has
not yet advanced to the point where the particulars of any given decision can be known
with certainty. Nonetheless, it is clear from facts already available that the real property
interests of some commissioners will be directly involved in some decisions, raising a
presumption that any financial effect will be material, disqualifying those commissioners
from any role in those decisions unless the presumption can be rebutted or one of the
Act's limited exceptions can be applied.

In these cases, unless and until it is possible to establish that a decision will have
no financial effect on the commissioner's real property interest, or that an exception to
the disqualification rule will apply, a commissioner whose real property interest will be

File No. 1-03-122
Page No. 9

directly involved in a decision should avoid any participation in discussions relating to
the decision in question. Long before a decisionmaking body is prepared to "make" a
decision, its members may "participate in making a decision" as described in regulation
18702.2. As pertinent here, subdivision (b) explains that a public official participates in
making a decision when, acting within the authority of his or her position, the official:

"(b) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, by:

(1) Conducting research or making any investigation which
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official
and the purpose of which is to influence a governmental
decision referenced in Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, section 18701(a)(2)(A); or

(2) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion,
orally, or in writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of
which is to influence a governmental decision referenced in
Title 2, California Code of Regulations, section
18701(a)(2)(A)."

"Policy discussions" among public officials typically require the exercise of
judgment and the presentation of opinions, and are often informed by some prior
investigation into the matters at issue. When such discussions are conducted by members
of a planning commission, whose ultimate function is to recommend action by the city
council, a planning commissioner will often find that he or she has "participated in the
making of a governmental decision" as soon as the "opinions" offered by the official in
such discussions can be said to have been given with the intent to influence the decisions
of the planning commission. This can happen at any stage of the planning process, since
the earliest "policy" decisions can shape all subsequent activities up to and including the
final vote in open session.

A planning commissioner who owns real property within 500 feet of a particular
"environmentally sensitive habitat area," or within a "dam inundation zone," or along a
particular railroad right of way, for example, should anticipate that when these particular
areas, zones, or rights-of-way are the subject of discussion by planning commissioners
and staff, at any stage of the planning process, participation in that discussion may well
amount to participation in a governmental decision directly involving the commissioner's
real property interest.

When the topic of discussion is more remote, when for example it concerns not a
particular area, zone, or right of way, but considers action on all such areas, zones, or
rights of way, the official will have to exercise his or her sound judgment – not only on
the subject matter of the discussion, but on whether he or she should participate at all.

File No. 1-03-122
Page No. 10

Section 87100 disallows participation when the official "knows or has reason to know"
that he or she has a financial interest in a decision. This is a commonsense standard
grounded in the facts and circumstances peculiar to a specific governmental decision.
There is no universal rule that will accurately instruct individual planners or commissioners
on the precise point at which he or she has "reason to know" that a decision is now on the
table in which he or she has a financial interest. The official must ultimately rely on his
or her informed judgment in these matters, a responsibility that cannot be altogether
eliminated from the conduct of public business.

With knowledge of the Act's requirements, and your specific planning needs, it
may be possible to structure the planning process in a manner that minimizes the number
of decisions in which one or more commissioners may not participate. We cannot advise
you in more detail on the optimal design of your planning process, an area beyond our
expertise, but we hope that a fuller understanding of the Act's requirements will assist
you in this effort.

If you have further questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

defined under the Act. Your first eight questions all refer to "decisions," and we presume
from this that the planning commissioners will at some point be faced with governmental
decisions relative to each of the topics you describe. Conflicts of interest are therefore
possible, and we may proceed with our analysis. o As to the "discussions" you expect to
take place as the planning process gets under way, participation in these discussions may
or may not amount to participation in govemmental decisions. But for present putposes
it is enough to know that, at some point in the process, the planning commissioners will
confront governmental decisions on the stated topics.
Step Three: What ore the possible sources of a conJlict of interestfor each officiat?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
mateial financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); regulation
1 8703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));
a
3 Regulation 18702.4lists a series ofexceptions to the rules stated in the preceding three
regulations, which apply only in certain limited circumstances.
a If an official has a conflict of interest in a particular decision, regulation 18702.5 explains that
the official is required to leave the room during discussion and voting on the matter in which tle ofhcial
has a conflict, unless the official wishes to be heard as a member of the general public, as provided in
subdivision (d)(3) of this regulation.
a
o
a
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation I5703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 871 03(c); regulation I 8703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
871 03(e); regulation 18706.fi;
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family - this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).
a
Your account of the facts includes information on various interests in real
property owned by seven of the planning commissioners. For purposes of this letter, we
assume that each of these commissioners has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 in
this real property. You do not suggest that the decisions in question would have a
personal financial effect separate and apart from any effect on the stated real property
interests. Accordingly, "personal financial effects" will not be treated separately from
financial effects on the commissioners' real property interests. (Regulation 18705.5(a).)
Our analysis will be limited, then, to the potential effects of decisionmaking on these real
property interests.s
Step Four: Are these economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision? .
Real property is directly involved in a govemmental decision if that real property
is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the property
which is the subject of the decision. (Regulation IS70a.2@)(1).) Your account of the
facts indicates that your own property is located within 500 feet of an environmentally
sensitive habitat area, unimplemented residential street plan lines, and a dam inundation
zone. Accordingly, your real property would be directly involved in governmental
decisions whose subjects include the areas you described.
Similarly, Commissioner McCarthy's property would be directly involved in
decisions on the described railroad right-of-way, proposed coastal access pathway, and
environmentally sensitive habitat area. Commissioner Kellenberger's property would be
directly involved in decisions on the environmentally sensitive habitat area to which you
refer. The property of Commissioners Ream and Poncini would be directly involved in
5 You have not reported any realproperfy interest owned by Planning Commissioner Falcone. But
since Commissioner Falcone does have an economic interest in his personal finances, it will be necessary to
keep in mind any personal financial effects that a decision may have on Commissioner Falcone. You have
not provided us any basis for the analysis of such effects on Commissioner Falcone, so we do not consider
them further in this letter.
decisions on the tsunami zones and environmentally sensitive habitat areas within 500
feet of their property. Finally, the property of Commissioner Heinzwould be directly
involved in decisions on the environmentally sensitive habitat area you described as
situated within 500 feet.
Real property not directly involved in a governmental decision is treated as
indirectly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18704.2(c)(2).) As we understand your
request, you do not seek assistance on decisions in which the commissioners' property is
indirectly involved. We limit our analysis, therefore, to the decisions you describe in
questions I through 8. In each of these the real property interest of one commissioner
(at least) is directly involved.
Steps Five and Six: What is the applicable materialifit standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon these oflicials,
economic interests will meet this materiality stundard?
Materiality
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) When real property is directly involved in a governmental
decision, there is a presumption that any financial effect of the decision upon the real
property is material. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(l) and (2).) This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have any
financial effect at all on the real property. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(l).)
The evidenee on which a public official relies to rebut the presumption rnust be
objectively defensible, that is, based on a full and accurate assessment of reasonable and
objective underlying facts. (Kuhn Advice Letter, No. 4-02-192; O'Harra Advice Letter,
No. A-00-174.) You have not furnished us with any evidence tending to rebut a pre-
sumption of materiality attaching to the properties found to be directly involved in the
decisions you mention. It may not be possible to provide such evidence at this early
stage; for the present, we must conclude that any financial effects on these properties,
attributable to the decisions you describe, would be material.
Foreseeablifit
An effect upon an economic interest is considered reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation I S706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (I975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) We have already concluded
(given our present state of knowledge) that arry financial effect of the decisions discussed
above, as to property directly involved in the decision, would be material. The question
now is, simply, whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions you have described
will have any frnancial effect on the directly involved real property. We cannot answer
that question because it necessarily depends on facts specific to each decision and to each
parcel of real property, which are not available to us at this time.
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions.
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in that decision if the circumstances are such that the "public generally"
exception may be invoked. This exception applies when the financial effect of a decision
upon a public official's economic interests is not distinguishable from the effect of the
decision on a significant segment of the public generally. (Section 87103; regulation
L8707(a).) We do not have information establishing that a "significant segment" of the
relevant population would be affected by the decision in "substantially the same manner,,
as the official is affected, in any of the decisions you describe, and cannot proceed fuither
in evaluating the possible impact of this exception.
Finally, an official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in that decision if the "legally required participation" exception is applicable.
This is an exception that typically applies when an agency is unable to assemble a
quorum of its members without the participation of an offrcial who has a conflict of
interest. You have not provided *y facts suggesting that this exception would apply to
any of the decisions you have described.
In summary, each of the decisions you describe appears to directly involve the
economic interests of at least one planning commissioner, who would have a conflict of
interest in the decision if it is foreseeable that the decision would have any financial
effect on the directly involved economic interest. At this early stage of the process, we
cannot say more than.that.
Question 9
Your final question asks how the planning commission should structure its
planning process to maximize participation of the planning commissioners while
avoiding conflicts of interest. This question presupposes that some commissioners will
have conflicts of interest in some decisions. As suggested by the foregoing analysis, that
is a reasonable supposition. It may be too early to state with certainty that agiven
commissioner will have a conflict of interest in a given decision, because the process has
not yet advanced to the point where the particulars of any given decision can be known
with certainty. Nonetheless, it is clear from facts already available that the real property
interests of some commissioners will be directly involved in some decisions, raising a
presumption that any frnancial effect will be material, disqualifiiing those commissioners
from any role in those decisions unless the presumption can be rebutted or one of the
Act's limited exceptions can be applied.
In these cases, unless and until it is possible to establish that a decision will have
no frnancial effect on the commissioner's real property interest, or that an exception to
the disqualification rule will apply, a commissioner whose real property interest will be
directly involved in a decision should avoid any participation in discussions relating to
the decision in question. Long before a decisionmaking body is prepared to "make" a
decision, its members may "pafiicipate in making a decision" as described in regulation
18702.2. As pertinent here, subdivision (b) explains that a public official participates in
making a decision when, acting within the authority of his or her position, the official:
"(b) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, by:
(1) Conducting research or making any investigation which
requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official
and the purpose of which is to influence a govemmental
decision referenced in Title 2, Califorrria Code of
Regulations, section 1 8701 (a)(2)(A); or
(2) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion,
orally, or in writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of
which is to influence a governmental decision referenced in
Title 2, Califomia Code of Regulations, section
18701(a)(zXA)."
"Policy discussions" among public officials typically require the exercise of
judgment and the presentation of opinions, and are often informed by some prior
investigation into the matters at issue. When such discussions are conducted by members
of a planning commission, whose ultimate function is to recomrnend action by the city
council, a planning commissioner will often find that he or she has "participated in the
making of a governmental decision" as soon as the "opinions" offered by the official in
such discussions can be said to have been given with the intent to influence the decisions
of the planning commission. This can happen at any stage of the planning process, since
the earliest "policy" decisions can shape all subsequent activities up to and including the
final vote in open session.
A planning commissioner who owns real property within 500 feet of a particular
"environmentally sensitive habitat area," or within a"dam inundation zone,,, or along a
particular railroad right of way, for example, should anticipate that when these particular
areas, zones, or rights-of-way are the subject of discussion byplanning commissioners
and staff, at any stage of the planning process, participation in that discussion may well
amount to participation in a governmental decision directly involving the commissioner's
real property interest.
When the topic of discussion is more remote, when for example it concems not a
particular area) zone, or right of way, but considers action on all such areas, zones, or
rights of way, the official will have to exercise his or her sound judgment - not only on
the subject matter of the discussion, but on whether he or she should participate at all.
Section 87i00 disallows participation when the official "knows or has reason to know"
that he or she has a financial interest in a decision. This is a commonsense standard
grounded in the facts and circumstances peculiar to a specific govefl)mental decision.
There is no universal rule that will acctrately instruct individual planning commissioners
on the precise point at which he or she has "reason to know" that adecision is now on the
table in which he or she has a financial interest. The official must ultimately rely on his
or her informed judgment in these matters, a responsibility that cannot be altogether
eliminated from the conduct of public business.
With knowledge of the Act's requirements, and your specific planning needs, it
may be possible to structure the planning process in a manner that minimizes the number
of decisions in which one or more commissioners may not participate. We cannot advise
you in more detail on the optimal design of your planning process, an area beyond our
expertise, but we hope that a fuller understanding of the Act's requirements will assist
you in this effort.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Does Commissioner McCarthy have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on vertical trail easements and coastal
access pathways within 500 feet of his residential real property?
2. 
Do Commissioners Benjamin, McCarthy, Kellenberger, Poncini, Ream andHeinz
have potential conflicts of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and
decisions on environmentally sensitive habitat areas within 500 feet of real property
owned by each commissioner?
3. 
Does Commissioner Benjamin have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on unimplemented residential street
plan lines within 500 feet of his residential real property?
I Government Code sections 8 1000 - 91014 . Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. ($ 831la; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
4. 
Does Commissioner Benjamin have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on dam inundation zones within 500
feet ofhis residential real property?
5. 
Does Commissioner McCarthy have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on the unused railroad right-of-way
passing through Half Moon Bay within 500 feet of his residential real property?
6. 
Do Commissioners Poncini and Ream have a potential conflict of interest
regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on land use in a tsunami
zone within which they own residential real properties?
7. 
Does Commissioner Poncini have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on development and land use within the
residential zoning district and LCP land use planning designations?
8. 
Does Commissioner Heinz have a potential conflict of interest regarding the
planning commission's discussions and decisions on development and land use within the
agricultural zoning district and LCP land use planning designations?
9. 
If one or more of the planning commissioners does have a potential conflict of
interest in the discussions and decisions described above, how should the planning
commission structure its planning process to maximize participation of the planning
commissioners while still adequately addressing conflict concerns?

CONCLUSION: Questions l-8
We presume that that the planning commissioners whose real property interests
are directly involved in the decisions you describe will have a conflict of interest in those
decisions, unless it can be shown that the decisions will have no frnancial effect on the
property interest, or that an exception to the conflict of interest rules would apply. From
the facts as presently known, it appears that the officials whose property interests would
be directly involved in these decisions would be prohibited from participating in them.
Therefore, at a minimum, they have "potential" conflicts of interest in these decisions.
Question 9
We cannot advise you on the optimal structure for the city's planning process,
which requires an understanding of goals and circumstances that can only be supplied by
city officials. To the extent that the legal requirements of the Act may be factors in the
design of your process, the analysis of the first 8 questions may be of some assistance.

Does Commissioner McCarthy have a potential conflict of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on vertical trail easements and coastal access pathways within 500 feet of his residential real property? 2. (§ 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) File No. I-03-122 Page No. 2 4. Does Commissioner Benjamin have a potential conflict of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on dam inundation zones within 500 feet of his residential real property? 5. Does Commissioner McCarthy have a potential conflict of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on the unused railroad right-of-way passing through Half Moon Bay within 500 feet of his residential real property? 6. Do Commissioners Poncini and Ream have a potential conflict of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on land use in a tsunami zone within which they own residential real properties? 7. Does Commissioner Poncini have a potential conflict of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on development and land use within the residential zoning district and LCP land use planning designations? 8. Does Commissioner Heinz have a potential conflict of interest regarding the planning commission's discussions and decisions on development and land use within the agricultural zoning district and LCP land use planning designations? 9. If one or more of the planning commissioners does have a potential conflict of interest in the discussions and decisions described above, how should the planning commission structure its planning process to maximize participation of the planning commissioners while still adequately addressing conflict concerns?
